text:
Cyclic molecular architectures, particularly those featuring medium-sized (7–11 membered) and macrocyclic (12+ membered) rings fused to aromatic systems, represent a privileged class of compounds in medicinal chemistry. Known as benzannulated medium-sized and macrocyclic rings (BMRs), these frameworks provide critical conformational constraints that enhance binding affinity, selectivity, and bioavailability, making them indispensable in the development of therapeutic agents for cancer, infectious diseases, and metabolic disorders. Despite their prevalence in potent natural products and clinically approved drugs, the synthesis of BMRs remains a formidable challenge due to unfavorable entropic factors and transannular interactions inherent in the formation of larger rings. 

This review provides a comprehensive overview of the biological significance of BMRs and examines the robust synthetic methodologies developed to access these complex scaffolds. We discuss established strategies such as lactonization, lactamization, and ring-closing metathesis (RCM), alongside transition-metal-catalyzed cross-couplings and oxidative ring expansions. Furthermore, specific attention is given to the use of carbohydrates as versatile chiral templates for the construction of enantiopure BMR ethers, thio-ethers, and lactones. By highlighting both naturally occurring and synthetic BMRs, this review underscores the ongoing importance of these cycles in drug discovery and the evolution of chemical strategies designed to overcome the hurdles of macrocyclization.
